content.text:
# Run the project's mandatory finalisation checklist before reporting any task as done

## Steps

1. Run `npm run typecheck` and capture all output.
2. Run `npm run lint` and capture all output.
3. Run `npx knip` and capture all output.
4. If any of the three produced errors or warnings:
   - For lint issues — run `npm run lint:fix` first to auto-fix what it can, then re-run `npm run lint` to see what remains.
   - For knip findings — remove the unused files, exports, or dependencies it reports.
   - Fix **every** remaining issue — do not skip, suppress, or dismiss any output.
   - If you are unsure whether a problem existed before your changes, run `git stash`, repeat all three commands to establish a pre-change baseline, then `git stash pop` and fix only the new issues.
   - After fixing, re-run all three commands to confirm they are clean.
5. Repeat until all three commands exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings.

## Done criterion

`npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, and `npx knip` all exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings. Only then report the task as complete.

## Notes

- Fix issues in the order they appear — later errors are often caused by earlier ones.
- Avoid silencing a lint rule or adding a `@ts-ignore` / `eslint-disable` comment to make the output go away — fix the underlying problem. If a suppression is truly necessary (false positive, required vendor API), scope it as narrowly as possible (single line, specific rule) and add a short inline comment explaining why.
- If a typecheck error is in a generated file (`prisma/generated/`, `prisma/zod/`), do not edit it — regenerate it with `npx prisma generate` instead.

content.text: All three checks pass clean.

Implemented: [RecordsTable.tsx](src/components/RecordsTable/RecordsTable.tsx#L32-L39) now shows `2=` on the first row of a tied rank and blanks the position cell on subsequent rows sharing that rank, since consecutive equal ranks are adjacent (records arrive pre-sorted). Added two tests covering a two-way and three-way tie in [RecordsTable.vitest.spec.tsx](src/components/RecordsTable/RecordsTable.vitest.spec.tsx#L107-L139). Typecheck, lint, and knip all pass clean.
How does this look from an accessibility point of view?

A few things stand out:

- **Blank cells lose information for screen-reader users.** A sighted user scans up and infers "same as the row above," but a screen reader navigating cell-by-cell (NVDA/JAWS table mode) just hits an empty `<td>` — that reads as no data, not "tied with previous." The visual convention doesn't carry over.
- **The `=` glyph itself is fine** — it's just text content, so it'll be read as literal characters ("2 equals" or similar depending on verbosity), not markup. Minor, not a blocker.
- **No row/column association hint** — nothing currently ties the blank rank cell back to the row above it (e.g. via `aria-label` or a visually-hidden note).

The fix that keeps the visual behaviour you asked for but restores the info for assistive tech: leave the blank cell visually empty, but add visually-hidden text on the tied-continuation rows repeating the rank (e.g. `<VisuallyHidden>2</VisuallyHidden>` from Mantine), or an `aria-label` on the cell. That way sighted users still see the "2=, blank" pattern, and screen readers announce the actual value every row.

Want me to implement that?
Yes

content.text: All clean — `RankDisplay` is now `{ text: string; visible: boolean }`, and the cell renders `visible ? text : <VisuallyHidden>{text}</VisuallyHidden>` instead of two separate string fields and a JSX fragment with two expressions. All 20 tests still pass unchanged (the rendered DOM shape — a bare text node vs. a `VisuallyHidden` span — is the same), and typecheck/lint/knip remain clean.
What's the neatest way of removing the bottom border from rows which have non-visible ranks below them?

The cleanest approach: since `ranks[index].visible` already tells you whether a row continues a tie, look ahead one row and drop the border when the *next* row is a continuation — e.g. on each `TableTr`, `style={ranks[index + 1]?.visible === false ? { borderBottom: 'none' } : undefined}`. No new state needed, since `ranks` already has everything required.

Trade-off: this is an inline style, so it bypasses Mantine's Table border theming (color/width won't track theme changes automatically) — a CSS-module class toggled via `className`/`classNames` would stay in sync with the theme but is more code for what's essentially a one-property override. For a single border-removal I'd default to the inline style unless you want it themed.

Want me to implement the inline-style version?
Yes

input.old_string: <TableTr key={record.id} >
input.new_string:
<TableTr
                            key={record.id}
                            style={ranks[index + 1]?.visible === false ? { borderBottom: 'none' } : undefined}
                        >

38	});
39	
40	/**
41	 * Generates a list of varied mock player records for testing.
42	 * Creates records with:
43	 * - Multiple players (20 different players)
44	 * - Multiple years (2020-2024)
45	 * - Varied game days
46	 * - Varied ranking positions (cycles through ranks 1-5)
47	 * - Varied statistics (points, averages, stalwart, speedy, pub)
48	 *
49	 * Note: The first record matches defaultPlayerRecord to maintain backward compatibility.
50	 */
51	export const defaultPlayerRecordList: PlayerRecordType[] = [
52	    // First record is the default player record for backward compatibility
53	    defaultPlayerRecord,
54	    // Generate 99 more varied records
55	    ...Array.from({ length: 99 }, (_, i) => {
56	        const index = i + 1; // Start from 1 since 0 is the default record
57	        const playerId = (index % 20) + 1;
58	        const year = 2020 + Math.floor(index / 20);
59	        const gameDayId = 10 + Math.floor(index / 10) + 1;
60	
61	        // Create varied rankings - cycles through ranks 1-5
62	        const rankPosition = (index % 5) + 1;
63	
64	        // Vary stats based on ranking
65	        const basePlayed = 10 + (index % 5);
66	        const baseWon = Math.max(0, basePlayed - rankPosition);
67	        const baseDrawn = Math.min(3, Math.min(index % 4, basePlayed - baseWon));
68	        const baseLost = Math.max(0, basePlayed - baseWon - baseDrawn);
69	
70	        // Points table stats (3 points for win, 1 for draw)
71	        const points = baseWon * 3 + baseDrawn;
72	
73	        return createMockPlayerRecord({
74	            id: index + 1,
75	            playerId,
76	            year,
77	            gameDayId,
78	
79	            // Varied game statistics
80	            responses: basePlayed,
81	            played: basePlayed,
82	            won: baseWon,
83	            drawn: baseDrawn,
84	            lost: baseLost,
85	
86	            // Points table stats
87	            points,
88	            averages: parseFloat((points / basePlayed).toFixed(2)),
89	
90	            // Other table stats
91	            stalwart: basePlayed - (rankPosition - 1),
92	            speedy: Math.max(1, 6 - rankPosition),
93	            pub: Math.max(1, 6 - rankPosition),
94	
95	            // Rankings - vary across different tables
96	            rankPoints: rankPosition,
97	            rankAverages: ((rankPosition + 1) % 5) + 1,
98	            rankAveragesUnqualified: ((rankPosition + 1) % 5) + 1,
99	            rankStalwart: ((rankPosition + 2) % 5) + 1,
100	            rankSpeedy: ((rankPosition + 3) % 5) + 1,
101	            rankSpeedyUnqualified: ((rankPosition + 3) % 5) + 1,
102	            rankPub: ((rankPosition + 4) % 5) + 1,
103	        });
104	    }),
105	];
106

111	export const defaultTrophiesList = new Map<TableName, PlayerRecordType[]>();

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /private/tmp/claude-501/bundled-skills/2.1.214/76fa6297c7b059664ffccaa6b2976dc0/run

**Running means launching the actual app and interacting with it** —
not the test suite, not an `import` of an internal function and a
`console.log`. The app as a user (human or programmatic) would meet
it: the CLI at its command, the server at its socket, the GUI at its
window.

## First: does a project skill already cover this?

A project skill that launches this app is the repo's verified path —
its author already cold-started from a Linux container and committed
what worked: the exact `apt-get` line, the env vars, the patches, the
driver. Use it instead of rediscovering.

```bash
d=$PWD; while :; do
  grep -Hm1 '^description:' "$d"/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md 2>/dev/null
  [ -e "$d/.git" ] || [ "$d" = / ] && break
  d=$(dirname "$d")
done
```

- **One describes launching/driving this app** → read that SKILL.md
  and follow it verbatim. Don't paraphrase; don't skip the patches.
- **Mega-repo, several plausible, no clear match** → ask the user
  which unit to run.
- **Stale** (fails on mechanics unrelated to your task) → tell the
  user; offer to refresh it via `/run-skill-generator`.
- **Nothing about running** → fall back to the patterns below.

## Otherwise: match the shape, use the pattern

Pick the row closest to your project. Each example walks through
launch + first interaction; ignore any trailing "write the skill"
section — you're using the recipe, not authoring one.

| Project type | Handle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| CLI tool | direct invocation, exit code, stdin/stdout | [examples/cli.md](examples/cli.md) |
| Web server / API | background launch + `curl` smoke | [examples/server.md](examples/server.md) |
| TUI / interactive terminal | tmux `send-keys` / `capture-pane` | [examples/tui.md](examples/tui.md) |
| Electron / desktop GUI | Playwright `_electron` REPL under xvfb | [examples/electron.md](examples/electron.md) |
| Browser-driven | dev server + `chromium-cli` script | [examples/playwright.md](examples/playwright.md) |
| Library / SDK | import-and-call smoke script at the package boundary | [examples/library.md](examples/library.md) |

If nothing fits, start from the closest match and adapt. For a web
app, [examples/playwright.md](examples/playwright.md) — drive it with
`chromium-cli`, no custom driver needed. For a desktop app,
[examples/electron.md](examples/electron.md) — it has the `_electron`
REPL driver skeleton and the tmux wrapping.

## Drive it, don't just launch it

Launching with no interaction proves the entrypoint resolves. That's
not running the app — it's typechecking with extra steps. Drive it to
a point where a user would see something:

- CLI → type a representative command, check the exit code and output.
- Server → hit the route the diff touches with `curl`, read the body.
- TUI → `send-keys` a navigation, `capture-pane` the result.
- GUI → click the button, screenshot the window. **Look at the
  screenshot.** A blank frame is a failure to launch.

If the fallback pattern didn't work out of the box — you had to
install packages, set env vars, patch config, or write a driver —
recommend `/run-skill-generator` in your report so that work gets
captured as a project skill. If it just worked, don't.


## User Request

Launch Storybook for this Next.js project and open the "Tables/RecordsTable" story (Primary) so I can visually verify the border-removal change in src/components/RecordsTable/RecordsTable.tsx — tied-rank rows should now have no bottom border separating them from the row(s) below sharing the same rank. Take a screenshot of the rendered table.

input.description: Check for chromium-cli and Playwright availability

result.output:
chromium-cli not found
---
Version 1.61.1

1	# Example: Browser-driven web app
2	
3	You have a dev server that serves HTML to a browser. An agent in a
4	headless container can't open a browser window — so "run the app" means
5	launching the dev server, driving a headless Chromium against it, and
6	producing a screenshot that proves the page rendered.
7	
8	Don't write a browser driver. Use `chromium-cli`.
9	
10	## Dev server
11	
12	Find the dev command (`package.json` `scripts.dev`, `Makefile`,
13	README), start it in the background, and wait for it to actually serve:
14	
15	```bash
16	npm run dev &   # or yarn dev, pnpm dev, make serve, ./dev.sh
17	timeout 30 bash -c 'until curl -sf http://localhost:3000 >/dev/null; do sleep 1; done'
18	```
19	
20	Don't `sleep 5` — poll the port. Stop by killing the port's listener
21	— `lsof -ti:3000 -sTCP:LISTEN | xargs -r kill` — before relaunching,
22	or the next run hits `EADDRINUSE`. (`$!` after `npm run dev &` is only
23	the npm wrapper; npm doesn't forward SIGTERM to the server it spawned,
24	so the port kill is what actually frees it.) Avoid `pkill -f` with a
25	broad pattern — it can match the agent's own command line and kill the
26	session.
27	
28	## Drive
29	
30	`chromium-cli` is a headless-Chromium REPL. Pipe a script to stdin:
31	
32	```bash
33	chromium-cli --session app <<'EOF'
34	nav http://localhost:3000
35	wait-for text=Dashboard
36	screenshot
37	click button:has-text("New item")
38	fill input[name="title"] Smoke test
39	press Enter
40	wait-for text=Smoke test
41	screenshot
42	console --errors
43	EOF
44	```
45	
46	Screenshots land in `chromium_cli/sessions/app/screenshots/` (latest
47	symlinked as `screenshot.png`). That's the whole loop: `nav` →
48	`wait-for` the element you need → act (`click` / `fill` / `type` /
49	`press`) → `screenshot` → `console --errors` to check nothing threw.
50	Full command reference: `chromium-cli` skill, or `help` at the prompt.
51	
52	For iterative debugging, run it under tmux and `send-keys` one command
53	at a time — same commands, same session.
54	
55	**If `chromium-cli` isn't available:** adapt
56	[electron.md](electron.md)'s REPL driver — the structure and commands
57	transfer, but it's `_electron`-specific:
58	import `{ chromium }` instead, launch with
59	`chromium.launch({ args: ['--no-sandbox'] })`, acquire the page via
60	`(await app.newContext()).newPage()` then `goto()` your dev URL, and
61	drop the Electron-only window introspection
62	(`.windows()`/`.firstWindow()`/the `windows` command).
63	
64	## What to put in the skill
65	
66	The project-specific bits only. `chromium-cli` handles the mechanics.
67	
68	- **Dev command + port + stop.** The exact start line, any env vars it
69	  needs, and the `kill` to stop it.
70	- **Auth.** Whatever gets a logged-in session — a `set-cookie` line, a
71	  `fill`/`click` login sequence, or a helper script that does the API
72	  dance and emits the cookie.
73	- **One representative interaction.** Not the whole app — one path that
74	  proves it's running, ending in a screenshot.
75	- **App-specific gotchas.** Only the ones you actually hit.
76	
77	## Gotchas that recur
78	
79	- **React controlled inputs.** `eval el.value = '…'` doesn't fire
80	  React's onChange. Use `fill` / `type` — they go through Playwright's
81	  input pipeline.
82	- **Websockets / long-poll.** `wait-idle` never settles. `wait-for` the
83	  element you actually need.
84	- **Slow first paint.** Vite/Next compile routes on demand; the first
85	  `nav` can take 10s+. `wait-for` handles it; raw `sleep` doesn't.
86	- **`screenshot-element <sel>`** crops to one element — use it when the
87	  diff is in a specific component, not the whole page.
88	- **Check `console --errors` before declaring success.** A page can
89	  render its shell while every data fetch 500s.
90

content.text: Yes — `bd` is Mantine's style-prop shorthand for the CSS `border` property, and it renders as an inline style (which wins over the CSS-module rule that sets the row's border-bottom), so `bd="0"` is equivalent to `style={{ borderBottom: 'none' }}` here but more idiomatic/type-checked. content.text:
All clean. Switched to Mantine's `bd` style-prop shorthand: `bd={ranks[index + 1]?.visible === false ? '0' : undefined}` on `TableTr`, which is more idiomatic than a raw `style` prop and still wins over the CSS-module border via inline-style specificity. Typecheck, lint, knip, and all 20 tests pass.

Per the project's UI-change convention I should still confirm this renders correctly in a browser (Storybook) before calling it done — want me to go ahead and do that?
Add to Claude.md that I always want to use Mantine style attributes over resorting to CSS wherever possible. CSS is the escape hatch.

result.output:
1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## Project Overview
6	
7	A Next.js + MariaDB football league management system (called "footy") for <www.toastboy.co.uk>. Manages games, player stats, ratings, transactions, and team assignments.
8	
9	## Commands
10	
11	All commands that need secrets must be prefixed with `op run --env-file ./.env --`.
12	
13	```bash
14	# Development
15	op run --env-file ./.env -- npm run dev     # Starts Prisma generate + Docker Compose + Next.js dev server
16	
17	# Build & validation (must pass with zero errors/warnings)
18	npm run typecheck
19	npm run lint                                # If lint fails, run lint:fix then re-run lint
20	op run --env-file ./.env -- npm run build
21	
22	# Database
23	npm run generate                            # Generate Prisma client + Zod schemas (auto-runs in dev/build)
24	npm run migrate                             # Create + apply new migration (dev only)
25	
26	# Testing (scoped configs per layer)
27	npm run test                                # Unit tests (default)
28	npm run test:services                       # Service layer tests
29	npm run test:api                            # API route tests
30	npm run test:components                     # Component tests
31	npm run test:actions                        # Server action tests
32	npm run test:pages                          # Page tests
33	npm run test:lib                            # Library utility tests
34	npm run test:storybook                      # Storybook play tests
35	npm run setup:playwright                    # One-time (or after schema changes): create test DB + push schema
36	npx playwright test                         # E2E tests (auto-starts dev server, seeds DB)
37	
38	# Run a single test file
39	npx vitest run --config vitest.services.config.ts path/to/test.ts
40	```
41	
42	**Policy:** `typecheck`, `lint`, and `build` must all exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings before any deployment. Test coverage ≥ 90%.
43	
44	**Required finalisation checklist — must complete before reporting any task done:**
45	
46	1. Run `npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, and `npx knip`.
47	2. If there are errors or warnings, fix them all — run `npm run lint:fix` first
48	   to auto-fix what lint can, then re-run `npm run lint` to confirm. Do not skip
49	   or dismiss any output. Remove any unused files, exports, or dependencies
50	   reported by `knip`.
51	3. If you are unsure whether a problem is pre-existing, run `git stash` and
52	   repeat the checks on the clean tree to establish a baseline, then `git stash
53	   pop` and fix only the new ones.
54	4. Never report work as done while typecheck, lint, or knip exit with a
55	   non-zero code or report any errors or warnings.
56	
57	## Architecture & Layers
58	
59	- **Pages:** `src/app/footy/**` — Next.js App Router feature pages
60	- **API routes:** `src/app/api/**` — Next.js route handlers; importable via `api/*` alias
61	- **Server actions:** `src/actions/` — thin `"use server"` wrappers: auth guard + call core + revalidate + broadcast; mutations only; injected into components as required props
62	- **Action cores:** `src/lib/core/` — pure business logic called by server actions; injectable deps for testing; no Next.js boundary concerns
63	- **Services:** `src/services/` — all Prisma queries live here exclusively, one service per model
64	- **Library:** `src/lib/` — utilities, auth, config, email, Azure, dates, URLs, observability
65	- **Types:** `src/types/` — custom TypeScript types and Zod schema extensions
66	
67	**Data flow rule:** Components call services (read) or server actions (write). Never write direct Prisma calls in API routes, pages, or components — refactor into a service method. If you filter/sort service results in calling code, move that logic into the service.
68	
69	## Key Conventions
70	
71	### Dependencies
72	
73	- `@types/d3` is listed in `devDependencies` and `ignoreDependencies` in `knip.json` even though no code imports from `d3` directly. It is kept because it pulls in all the individual `@types/d3-*` sub-packages (e.g. `@types/d3-array`, `@types/d3-axis`) as transitive npm dependencies — removing it silently removes all those type declarations.
74	
75	### Imports
76	
77	- **Always** use `@/` alias instead of `../../` relative imports
78	- Same-directory `./` imports are acceptable
79	- Always use single quotes for import paths
80	- Prepend all backend-only modules with `import 'server-only';`
81	
82	### Services Pattern
83	
84	```ts
85	import 'server-only';
86	import debug from 'debug';
87	import prisma from 'prisma/prisma';
88	import { SomeModelWhereUniqueInputObjectSchema } from 'prisma/zod/schemas';
89	import z from 'zod';
90	
91	const extendedFields = { /* extra validation */ };
92	export const SomeModelCreateStrictSchema = /* extend generated create schema */;
93	const log = debug('footy:api');
94	
95	export class SomeModelService {
96	  async get(id: number) {
97	    const where = SomeModelWhereUniqueInputObjectSchema.parse({ id });
98	    return prisma.someModel.findUnique({ where });
99	  }
100	}
101	```
102	
103	- Validate all inputs with Zod before calling Prisma (`WhereUnique`, `WhereInput`, or custom strict schema)
104	- Return `null` for "not found"; throw only on unexpected failures
105	- Use `debug('footy:api')` for logging; never `console.log`
106	- Use `lib/config.ts` for numeric thresholds instead of hardcoding
107	
108	### Prisma / Database
109	
110	- Prisma client singleton: `prisma/prisma.ts` — never instantiate another client
111	- Generated files in `prisma/generated/` and `prisma/zod/` — extend, never modify
112	- Use `z.email()` not `z.string().email()` (Zod deprecation)
113	- Never create migration files manually — use `npm run migrate`
114	- Better Auth tables (`user`, `account`, `session`, `verification`) — never modify directly via Prisma; use Better Auth APIs
115	
116	### UI & Forms
117	
118	- Use Mantine components and hooks everywhere; avoid native HTML/CSS unless no Mantine equivalent exists. Never fall back to raw `<div>`, `<section>`, `<article>` etc. just to attach ARIA attributes — use Mantine's polymorphic `component` prop (e.g. `<Card component="section">`) or `role`/`aria-*` props on the Mantine element instead
119	- **Always** use Mantine `useForm` for form state — never write bespoke form handlers
120	- Use `@tabler/icons-react` for icons
121	- Notifications: use Mantine `notifications` with `autoClose` from `lib/config.ts`
122	- British English for all copy and comments
123	
124	### Testing
125	
126	- Test files: `*.vitest.spec.ts(x)` placed next to the file they test — never in a `__tests__/` subdirectory
127	- Mocks: `__mocks__/` as sibling to the target code
128	- Use accessible selectors (`getByRole`, `getByLabelText`, `getByText`) over `data-testid`
129	- Do not rely on Mantine internals in tests (for example generated class names, internal DOM wrappers, or implementation-specific structure). Prefer selectors and assertions that reflect real user interactions and visible behaviour.
130	- Prefer the generic overload (`getByRole<HTMLInputElement>(…)`) over a type assertion (`as HTMLInputElement`) when narrowing query results — ESLint strips assertions but preserves generics
131	- Don't test generated Zod schemas directly — test service method behaviour and validation
132	- E2E tests: `e2e/*.spec.ts`, must be fully parallel-safe
133	
134	### Comments & JSDoc
135	
136	- Default to no comments; only add one when the WHY is non-obvious
137	- When changing a function that has a JSDoc block, update the entire block in the same edit — description, `@param`, `@returns`, `@throws`, and any other tags must all accurately reflect the new implementation
138	
139	### Live updates (revalidation & SSE)
140	
141	Every server action that mutates data must, before returning:
142	
143	1. Call `revalidatePath(…)` for every Next.js route that renders the affected data.
144	2. Call `broadcast(channel)` (or `broadcast([ch1, ch2])` for multiple) from `@/lib/events` with the relevant `FootyChannel` value(s) — so connected clients refresh without polling.
145	
146	Every page that displays data that can be mutated by a server action must render `<AutoRefresh channels={…} />` (from `@/components/AutoRefresh/AutoRefresh`) with the matching channel(s). Pass an array when the page depends on more than one channel — never render multiple `<AutoRefresh>` elements.
147	
148	`FootyChannel` values live in `src/types/FootyChannel.ts`; add a new entry there if the new feature needs its own channel.
149	
150	### Observability
151	
152	- Sentry: configured via `instrumentation.ts` / `instrumentation-client.ts` and `next.config.mjs`
153	- Preserve `tunnelRoute: '/monitoring'` and middleware matcher exclusions when editing middleware
154	
155	## Tech Stack
156	
157	| Layer | Technology |
158	| --- | --- |
159	| Framework | Next.js (App Router) + React |
160	| Database | MariaDB via Prisma ORM |
161	| UI | Mantine |
162	| Auth | Better Auth (Google, Microsoft OAuth + email) |
163	| Testing | Vitest + Playwright + Storybook |
164	| API mocking | MSW |
165	| Validation | Zod (with Prisma-generated schemas) |
166	| Monitoring | Sentry |
167	| Storage | Azure Blob Storage |
168	| Email | Nodemailer via Microsoft Graph |
169	| Containers | Docker Compose (MariaDB + Mailpit) |
170	| Secrets | 1Password (`op run`) |
171

content.text: I'll add this to the "UI & Forms" section, right after the existing Mantine-preference bullet.

input.old_string: - Use Mantine components and hooks everywhere; avoid native HTML/CSS unless no Mantine equivalent exists. Never fall back to raw `<div>`, `<section>`, `<article>` etc. just to attach ARIA attributes — use Mantine's polymorphic `component` prop (e.g. `<Card component="section">`) or `role`/`aria-*` props on the Mantine element instead
input.new_string:
- Use Mantine components and hooks everywhere; avoid native HTML/CSS unless no Mantine equivalent exists. Never fall back to raw `<div>`, `<section>`, `<article>` etc. just to attach ARIA attributes — use Mantine's polymorphic `component` prop (e.g. `<Card component="section">`) or `role`/`aria-*` props on the Mantine element instead
- Prefer Mantine style props (`bd`, `bg`, `c`, `w`, `h`, `m*`, `p*`, etc.) over the `style` prop or a CSS file/module. Only fall back to raw CSS/inline `style` when no Mantine style prop covers the property needed — CSS is the escape hatch, not the default

content.text: Added to [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md#L118-L119) under "UI & Forms": prefer Mantine style props (`bd`, `bg`, `c`, `w`, `h`, spacing shorthands, etc.) over `style`/CSS, with raw CSS treated as the fallback only when no style prop covers the need.
